planting.note: Start this F1 seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly in warm, moist medium and thin to the strongest seedlings once they have a few leaves. This seed line flowers early and uniformly, making it a good choice for spring bedding grown as an annual. Set plants out after frost and remove faded white blooms to prolong the show.

planting.note: Start this F1 seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost.

directions: Start seed indoors in a warm, bright spot and keep the surface just moist until germination. Grow the seedlings cool and steady, then plant them out after the last frost in full sun. The rich purple daisies bloom early and continue with regular removal of spent flowers.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Transplant into full sun in freely draining soil after frost danger has passed. Clear yellow daisies with dark eyes cover a tidy, mounding plant well suited to pots. Deadhead and feed lightly to keep the flowers coming through the cooler stretches of the season.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant into a sunny, well-drained spot once nights stay reliably mild. This is a heat-tolerant, early-blooming type that keeps flowering longer into summer than many. Rich purple daisies over dark centers respond well to deadheading and a light monthly feed.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Set out plants after frost in full sun and firm the soil around the roots. Crisp white petals ring a blue-toned central disk on a compact, free-flowering plant. Remove spent blooms and avoid overwatering to keep it healthy and in flower.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in full sun in sharply drained soil once the weather has settled. Blooms open in coppery amethyst tones that shift with temperature for a multi-hued effect. Deadhead regularly and keep the roots out of standing water for the best performance.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Transplant into a sunny, freely draining bed or container after frost. This vigorous, well-branched type flowers heavily and holds up in warm spells better than average. Pinch spent flowers and feed monthly to keep the clean white daisies coming.

planting.note: Broadcast seed in autumn or very early spring where plants are to flower.

directions: Mix the fine seed with a little sand and sprinkle it over raked soil, then firm it in without burying it. Thin seedlings to a hand's width apart and water sparingly once established. This selection offers softer pastel and picotee shades with pale centers, blooming across early to midsummer.

planting.note: Sow directly in early spring or late autumn for the earliest bloom.

directions: Surface-sow the seed onto firmed soil and keep it lightly moist until germination, giving no cover. Space plants apart as they grow so air moves freely between them. This strain produces fully double, ruffled blooms in a pastel range from cream to smoky lavender through early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow onto bare soil in autumn or early spring; this old strain is also called Fairy Wings.

directions: Scatter the fine seed thinly on the surface and press gently, leaving it exposed to light. Thin the seedlings and let the plants dry between waterings. Blooms come in muted dove-grey, dusky mauve, and soft peach with subtle speckling, appearing in early summer.

planting.note: Broadcast over open ground in early spring or after the first fall frosts.

directions: Sow the seed on the surface of firmed soil and do not cover it, watering with a fine mist to avoid washing it away. Thin to give each plant room and keep the site lean and sunny. This is the classic single vivid-red memorial poppy, flowering freely in early summer.

planting.note: Sow directly onto cold soil in early spring for strong pods.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of firm ground and mist gently, giving no cover as light aids sprouting. Thin the seedlings well apart to encourage large heads. Lavender-pink single flowers give way to fat pods full of slate-blue culinary seed by late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in earliest spring; will not transplant well.

directions: Press the fine seed lightly onto damp soil without burying it and keep evenly moist until germination. Thin plants generously so stems stand strong. This selection is loved for its deep single blooms in a uniform saturated grape-purple through midsummer.

planting.note: Sow on the surface of cool spring soil where plants will flower.

directions: Broadcast the seed thinly and leave it exposed to light, watering with a gentle mist. Thin seedlings to a good spacing so each fringed flower can develop. Blooms are bright red with a bold white cross at the center, opening in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Direct sow early; these peony-flowered types resent any root disturbance.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on firmed soil and keep barely moist until it sprouts, giving no cover. Thin the young plants so air circulates and stems stay upright. The fully double, ruffled blooms are deep maroon-black and appear in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow directly in early spring for the largest seed pods.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface and press lightly, watering with a mist until seedlings emerge. Thin to give each plant space to form its large pod. Deep mauve-red single flowers are followed by big rounded seed heads excellent for drying and arrangements.

planting.note: Direct sow onto cool spring soil; do not start indoors.

directions: Sprinkle seed thinly over firmed earth and leave uncovered, misting to settle it in. Thin the seedlings well so plants branch and stand tall. Large, full pompon-double blooms in clean white open across midsummer above blue-green foliage.

planting.note: Sow in place in early spring; this type makes the largest pods of all.

directions: Surface-sow the seed onto raked ground and keep lightly damp until germination, adding no soil cover. Thin plants to a generous spacing so each develops a heavy head. Tall stems carry large mauve flowers followed by oversized seed pods valued for dried displays.

planting.note: Direct sow onto cold spring soil where the plants are to bloom.

directions: Broadcast the fine seed thinly on the surface and firm it in without covering. Thin seedlings so each has room to form a full double flower. The ruffled peony-style blooms are streaked rose-pink and white, opening in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Sow directly in early spring; grown for white culinary seed.

directions: Scatter seed thinly over firmed soil and leave it uncovered, misting gently until it sprouts. Thin the plants to encourage strong single stems. This old Czech variety bears pale single flowers and pods that ripen white baking seed by late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring on the surface of prepared soil.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto damp soil without covering and keep it lightly moist until seedlings appear. Thin the plants and grow them lean in full sun. The compact scarlet cups each carry a bold black blotch, resembling a ladybird, and flower through early summer.

planting.note: Start on the surface in cool conditions; performs best in mild spring and fall weather.

directions: Sprinkle the very fine seed onto moist firmed soil and leave it uncovered under bright light. Keep the surface damp while it germinates and thin the seedlings to a good spacing. This compact strain produces large blooms in warm sherbet tones and holds up well as a cut flower.

planting.note: Sow on the soil surface in early spring or late summer for cool-season bloom.

directions: Press the fine seed lightly onto damp soil and leave exposed to light, keeping it evenly moist until sprouts show. Thin to space the low rosettes and grow in cool, bright conditions. This dwarf series flowers in clear white, yellow, orange, and pink on short sturdy stems, ideal for pots and edging.

planting.note: Surface-sow in cool spring or fall; a compact strain for containers.

directions: Scatter the dust-like seed on firmed moist soil and do not cover it, misting to keep it damp. Thin the seedlings so each low plant has room. This dwarf mix blooms in a bright range of yellow, orange, salmon, and white, flowering over a long cool-season stretch.

planting.note: Direct sow onto the surface in early spring where plants will flower.

directions: Press the fine seed onto firmed soil without burying it and keep barely moist until germination. Thin the plants and grow them in lean, well-drained ground with plenty of sun. The bright red cup-shaped blooms sit above blue-grey foliage and look strikingly like tulips in early summer.

planting.note: Sow directly onto cool spring soil; a wild relative of the breadseed poppy.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface of firmed soil and leave it uncovered, misting to settle it. Thin the seedlings so each stem can form its small pod. Lilac single flowers with dark bases give way to compact bristly seed heads by late summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring on the surface of open ground.

directions: Mix the fine seed with sand and broadcast it over firmed soil, leaving it uncovered for light. Thin seedlings to a hand's width and water sparingly. The ruffled semi-double to double blooms come in pinks, salmon, and white with paler edges through early summer.

planting.note: Sow directly onto the surface in autumn or very early spring.

directions: Sprinkle the tiny seed over firmed soil and press it in lightly without covering. Thin the plants for good airflow and keep the ground on the dry side. This mix offers bicolor and picotee blooms in shades of rose, salmon, and cream, many semi-double, flowering in early summer.

planting.note: Direct sow in earliest spring; these double forms will not transplant.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on firmed soil and keep barely damp until it germinates, giving no cover. Thin plants generously so each forms a large full head. The peony-flowered doubles bloom in rich reds, pinks, purples, and white during mid to late summer.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors under warmth well ahead of the last frost for early bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly into a sterile mix and barely cover, holding steady warmth until germination. Grow on in bright light and set out sturdy plants after the weather settles, spacing for good air movement. This early, uniform strain flowers reliably in its first season and rewards regular deadheading with waves of large round heads.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter so plants are budded by planting time.

directions: Start seed in warm trays, covering only lightly, and provide bright light once seedlings emerge to keep them compact. Transplant to the garden or containers after frost, working in a balanced feed. Well-branched plants carry big flower balls above dark-zoned leaves all summer if you remove faded heads.

planting.note: This quick strain needs a slightly shorter head start than most seed geraniums.

directions: Sow shallowly in a warm, bright spot and grow seedlings on without letting them stretch. Harden off and plant out after the last frost, spacing closely for a solid carpet of color. Bred for very heavy flowering from many small heads at once, it needs only occasional grooming to stay tidy.

planting.note: Give this vigorous strain an early indoor start for full-size plants by summer.

directions: Barely cover seed in warm, moist mix and keep humidity high until sprouting. Pot up and grow bright, then set out after frost in fertile, well-drained soil. This early, base-branching strain fills beds and boxes quickly and blooms steadily when spent heads are pinched off.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for large, well-branched plants at planting time.

directions: Start seed warm and shallow, then grow seedlings in strong light to avoid stretch. Transplant after frost into rich soil and feed lightly through the season. Large-flowered and consistent, this strain performs in both beds and pots, blooming continuously with routine deadheading.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early to enjoy both the dark foliage and flowers by summer.

directions: Sow warm and cover only lightly, giving bright light so the near-black leaf color develops. Set plants out after frost in full sun, which deepens the foliage tone. Bright flower heads contrast against the dark leaves and keep coming when old blooms are removed.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants once nights stay reliably above frost.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the same depth they grew in the pot, into fertile, well-drained soil. Water at the base to keep foliage dry and feed lightly every few weeks. This robust series holds large, weather-tolerant heads on strong stems and blooms hardest with steady deadheading.

planting.note: Move rooted plants outdoors after the last frost into full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in rich, free-draining soil and give them full sun for the best flower count. This vigorous cross between zonal and ivy types is heat-tolerant and sterile, so it pours energy into velvety dark-red blooms instead of seed. Trim off fading heads to keep the mounded plant flowering from spring until frost.

planting.note: Set out after frost where plants get sun and can spread a bit.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into well-drained soil or containers and grow in full sun. This semi-trailing zonal-ivy hybrid tolerates heat and humidity and self-cleans fairly well. It mounds and spills over pot edges with continuous color through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in baskets or boxes where stems can cascade.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into free-draining potting mix at the rim of a basket or window box. The fleshy, ivy-shaped leaves resist wilting, so let the soil dry slightly and avoid overhead watering. Long trailing stems carry loose flower clusters and bloom best with some shelter from harsh midday heat.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings into baskets after frost for a trailing display.

directions: Plant into a light, well-drained mix and let stems spill over the container edge. Keep foliage dry and allow the surface to dry between waterings to prevent rot. This compact, well-branched ivy strain flowers heavily along cascading stems all season.

planting.note: Set out after frost in raised boxes so the long stems can trail freely.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in free-draining containers and water at the base as the surface dries. Famous on European balconies, this vigorous trailer sends out very long stems smothered in small flowers. Give it light feeding and it will cascade several feet by late summer.

planting.note: Grow in pots and set outside after frost, ideally with afternoon shade.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in a fertile, well-drained mix and grow on the cool, bright side. Often called Martha Washington geranium, it flowers most freely after cool spring nights and can pause in high summer heat. Deadhead the large, ruffled blooms and shelter plants from scorching afternoon sun.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a bright spot to bring out the leaf coloring.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and give strong light so the leaves keep their green, bronze, and cream banding. Grown mainly for its striking tricolor foliage, it also carries small orange-red flowers. Pinch stem tips to keep the plant bushy and colorful.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun for the deepest leaf color.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into free-draining soil and grow in bright sun to intensify the bronze-and-gold butterfly-marked leaves. Small star-shaped orange-red flowers rise above the striking foliage. Keep it compact by pinching and remove old blooms to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into bright light to develop the leaf pattern.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and provide plenty of light for the gold, green, and red leaf zoning to show. This classic tricolor is grown chiefly for foliage but also bears soft salmon-pink flowers. Pinch regularly to build a full, well-shaped plant.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sun for crisp cream leaf edges.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in a light, well-drained mix and grow in bright conditions. This tidy, compact plant pairs green-and-cream variegated leaves with delicate salmon-pink single flowers. Its neat habit suits small pots and edging, needing only light pinching and deadheading.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light to keep the leaves golden.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in free-draining soil and give good light so the yellow-green foliage stays bright with its central green splash. Loose red flowers rise above the golden leaves. Pinch to shape and remove faded heads to extend the show.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in bright light; this novelty grows a bit slowly.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and grow in strong light. Each leaf opens with a bold white center ringed in green, an old novelty prized for foliage over its red flowers. Because the pale centers carry little chlorophyll, grow it steadily and avoid overwatering.

planting.note: Set out after frost in a sunny, well-drained spot with room to grow.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in gritty, well-drained soil and let it dry well between waterings. The deeply cut leaves release a strong rose fragrance when brushed and can be used in cooking or potpourri. Small pink flowers appear in summer, but pinch stems often to keep the aromatic bush full.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sun and sharp-draining soil.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into gritty soil and water sparingly once established. The soft, lobed leaves carry a rich rose scent and are a favorite for flavoring sugar, jelly, and baked goods. Small mauve-pink flowers dot the plant in summer; trim regularly to harvest leaves and keep it bushy.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in bright light to hold the cream leaf edges.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and provide plenty of light to keep the leaf margins creamy white. The finely cut, rose-scented foliage is decorative in pots and useful for potpourri. Let the soil dry between waterings and pinch tips to maintain the variegated, mounded form.

planting.note: Set out after frost in sun; its upright habit suits containers.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in gritty, free-draining soil and water modestly. The small, crinkled leaves are packed along stiff upright stems and give off a sharp lemon scent when touched. Trim to shape the columnar plant and enjoy occasional small pink flowers in summer.

planting.note: Grow in a pot and set outdoors after frost in a sunny spot.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in sharp-draining soil and keep it on the dry side. The rough, deeply cut leaves carry one of the strongest lemon fragrances of any scented geranium. Give it warmth and light, pinch to shape, and enjoy small lilac flowers in summer.

planting.note: Set out after frost where it has room to sprawl; tolerates some shade.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and give room, since this species spreads widely. The large, velvety-soft leaves smell strongly of peppermint and prefer a little shade from harsh sun. Water moderately and trim the sprawling stems to keep it in bounds.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a spot with some afternoon shade.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in well-drained soil where the plant can spread. Its large, soft leaves show a dark chocolate-brown blotch in the center and smell of peppermint. Give partial shade and moderate water, trimming the trailing stems to keep the mound tidy.

planting.note: Set out after frost in sun; its small size suits pots and edging.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in gritty soil and let it dry between waterings. The small gray-green leaves smell warmly of nutmeg and spice when rubbed. This compact, spreading plant carries tiny white flowers and stays neat with occasional pinching.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and water sparingly once rooted in. The dark, oak-shaped leaves are sticky and pungently scented, with attractive markings along the veins. Pink flowers appear in summer; trim to keep this upright, shrubby plant compact.

planting.note: Set out after frost; its low, spreading habit suits baskets.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and water lightly. The soft, round, apple-green leaves give off a fresh green-apple scent and trail gently over pot edges. Small white flowers appear on slender stems; pinch to keep the low mound full.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sun; keeps a compact upright shape.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in gritty, well-drained soil and let it dry between waterings. The small, slightly crinkled leaves release a bright orange-citrus scent when touched. Grow in good light, pinch to shape, and enjoy pale mauve flowers in summer.

planting.note: Move rooted plants outdoors after frost into full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in fertile, well-drained soil and grow in full sun. This antique zonal carries clusters of double buds that never fully open, resembling tiny pink-and-white rosebuds. Because the blooms rarely shatter, simply remove whole heads as they fade to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sun; its dwarf habit suits small pots.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and grow in bright light. This compact stellar type has narrow, pointed leaves with dark zoning and star-shaped, spidery pink flowers. Its small size makes it ideal for windowsills and edging; deadhead to keep blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in sharp-draining soil and full sun.

directions: Set plants in gritty, very well-drained soil and water sparingly, as the thick roots resent wet feet. It forms a low rosette of silvery, heart-shaped leaves above which thin stems carry small, near-black maroon flowers. Give it full sun and lean conditions, and protect from frost or overwinter indoors.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun; give it room as a shrubby plant.

directions: Set plants in well-drained soil in full sun and water sparingly once established. A parent of many regal geraniums, this South African shrub bears cupped, hairy leaves and showy purple-pink flowers in spring. Prune after flowering to keep the tall, woody plant shapely.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Set plants in well-drained soil in a sunny spot and water modestly. This wild South African species is a key ancestor of garden zonal geraniums, with rounded leaves often marked by a faint darker ring. It carries loose clusters of pink flowers and grows into a tall, somewhat lax plant; prune to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Set out after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and water sparingly. Another ancestor of modern bedding geraniums, this species has soft, rounded leaves without a strong zone and bears bright scarlet flowers. Prune the tall, branching stems after bloom to maintain a fuller shape.

planting.note: Set out after frost; its trailing habit suits baskets and edges.

directions: Plant in well-drained soil where the low stems can trail over an edge. The small rounded leaves carry a faint coconut scent, and tiny magenta flowers appear on wiry stems. It self-seeds readily, so trim spent stems if you want to limit its spread.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny spot with good drainage.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in well-drained soil and water modestly. The deeply cut leaves give off a lemony, citronella-like scent when brushed, and the plant is often sold as a mosquito deterrent. Grow it in full sun, pinch to keep it bushy, and enjoy occasional pale pink flowers.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about ten weeks early and move plants out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of moist mix without covering it, since light aids sprouting. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching and feed regularly through summer. Remove faded blooms to keep the large, ruffled flowers coming until frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it warm and evenly damp until it sprouts. These plants carry many smaller, weather-tough blooms, so they bounce back well after rain. Deadhead lightly and shear midseason if growth gets leggy for a fresh flush of flowers.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and transplant to warm garden soil or containers after frost.

directions: Sprinkle seed on the surface of moist starting mix and leave it uncovered under bright light. This compact type covers itself in miniature blooms and needs little pinching to stay tidy. Water steadily in heat and expect nonstop color in small pots and edging.

planting.note: Start indoors early or buy transplants, then plant out once nights stay mild.

directions: Give these vigorous plants room to run, as each one can blanket several feet of bed or spill from a basket. Feed heavily and water often, since fast growth is hungry and thirsty. Trim wayward stems if the center thins, and enjoy a low carpet of bloom all season.

planting.note: Sow indoors well before the last frost, then transplant to full sun after it passes.

directions: Set plants in rich soil and space them wide so the low, spreading stems can knit into a magenta groundcover. Feed steadily and water in dry spells to keep the flush of self-cleaning blooms going. Expect it to trail over walls or fill a basket without deadheading.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out to sun after frost for mounded, trailing color.

directions: Surface-sow the seed under light and grow warm until sprouts appear. This selection mounds more than the original Wave, making it tidy in beds and baskets alike. Water and feed regularly and it will pour out bright red blooms until hard frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors in late winter and transplant after the frost date.

directions: Press the seed onto damp mix and leave uncovered for light. This small-flowered spreading type recovers fast from summer storms and stays full without pinching. Keep it fed and watered in containers for an early, dense show of little blooms.

planting.note: Start indoors early; give each plant plenty of space when setting out after frost.

directions: Sow on the surface and grow warm and bright until seedlings size up. Spaced apart it stays low and spreading, but planted close it climbs into a tall hedge of silvery-lavender bloom. Feed generously and water in heat to fuel its strong growth all summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: This vegetative cultivar is grown from cuttings rather than seed, so start with nursery plants. Feed it steadily and water often, since it grows fast and blooms hard through heat. It never needs deadheading and mounds and trails with pink bloom until frost.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings into warm beds or baskets after frost.

directions: Buy this cutting-grown selection as young plants, since it does not come true from seed. Give it full sun, regular food, and steady moisture for the deepest color. Its rich purple, self-cleaning blooms cover the plant nonstop with no pinching required.

planting.note: Transplant rooted plants after frost into full-sun containers or beds.

directions: Start with nursery plants of this vigorous cutting-grown cultivar. Feed and water it heavily to support its large, fast-growing habit through summer. Soft silver-pink blooms with darker veins keep coming without any deadheading.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost; cool nights sharpen the speckling.

directions: This cutting-grown novelty is sold as plants, not seed. Its purple blooms are dappled with white spots, and the pattern shows best when nights are cool and days are bright. Feed regularly and keep evenly watered for a steady show through the season.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost into rich, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow this double-flowered selection from purchased plants, as it is propagated by cuttings. The ruffled lavender blooms are heavy, so shelter from hard rain keeps them looking their best. Feed steadily and pinch spent flowers to keep the fully double display fresh.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out after frost into baskets or beds in full sun.

directions: This trailing group is always grown from cuttings, so begin with nursery stock. The long, cascading stems are made for hanging baskets and window boxes. Feed often and water daily in summer heat for a waterfall of self-cleaning bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and move out after frost; it self-sows in mild gardens.

directions: Surface-sow this rare species and keep it warm and bright to germinate. It is one of the few truly red, hummingbird-pollinated petunias and has an open, upright habit. Give it sun and steady moisture, and it will bloom scarlet through the warm months.

planting.note: Sow indoors and transplant after frost to a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and leave it uncovered under light. This wild parent of garden petunias bears fragrant white trumpets that open toward evening for moths. Grow it in sun with even water and enjoy the sweet night scent.

planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out after frost; it sprawls readily in warm soil.

directions: Surface-sow and germinate warm and bright. This low, spreading wild species is a key ancestor of trailing garden petunias and carries small violet blooms freely. Give it full sun and moderate water for a loose, mounding carpet of color.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant to sun after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and moist under bright light. This grandiflora holds an unusually clear soft-yellow color that fades to cream as blooms age. Deadhead and feed through summer to keep the large flowers coming.

planting.note: Start indoors early and set out after frost for weather-tolerant bedding color.

directions: Press seed onto damp mix and leave uncovered for light to germinate. This compact multiflora series shrugs off rain and heat and comes in a wide color range. Pinch once when young and deadhead to keep the mounded plants blooming.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost into slightly acidic, well-drained mix.

directions: Most million bells are grown from cuttings, so begin with nursery plants set into acidic potting mix. They are heavy feeders that flower best with regular fertilizer and good drainage, so avoid soggy soil. The little petunia-like bells self-clean and pour over pot edges all summer.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out after frost into full sun and acidic mix.

directions: Buy this cutting-grown cultivar as plants since it will not come from seed. Its cherry-pink blooms carry a bright yellow star pattern that shows best in sun. Feed regularly, water evenly, and let it trail without any deadheading.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings after frost into well-drained containers.

directions: Start with nursery plants of this vegetative selection. The blooms show a bold pinwheel of yellow and white that reads clearly from a distance. Keep it fed and evenly moist in acidic mix for nonstop, self-cleaning color.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost into sun and acidic soil.

directions: This cutting-grown cultivar is sold as plants for containers and baskets. Its purple blooms carry a dark eye that deepens the color in full sun. Feed steadily and provide sharp drainage for a summer-long cascade of bloom.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost into full sun and free-draining mix.

directions: Grow this solid-yellow selection from purchased cuttings, not seed. Give it full sun, acidic mix, and steady feeding for the brightest flower color. It trails freely and needs no deadheading to bloom until frost.

planting.note: Transplant rooted plants after frost into sun and acidic potting mix.

directions: This vegetative cultivar comes as plants for pots and baskets. Its deep red blooms carry a near-black center that gives the flower rich contrast. Feed regularly and keep it evenly moist but never waterlogged for lasting color.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost into sunny baskets or beds.

directions: Begin with nursery plants of this cutting-grown trailing type. The violet-blue bells cover long cascading stems that spill well from hanging baskets. Feed steadily, use acidic mix, and water evenly for months of self-cleaning bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early, as this is the seed-grown calibrachoa; plant out after frost.

directions: Unlike most calibrachoa, this series grows from seed, so surface-sow it warm under bright light. Move seedlings to acidic, well-drained mix and set them out once frost has passed. Feed regularly and it blooms in miniature petunia-like bells all summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into sun and acidic, well-drained mix.

directions: This cutting-grown series is sold as plants in a broad color range. It holds a slightly more mounded, well-branched habit that suits mixed containers. Feed often, keep the mix acidic and moist, and enjoy dense bloom with no deadheading.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost; it favors moist, sandy ground.

directions: Surface-sow this small wild species and germinate it warm and bright. A low, spreading annual of damp sandy sites, it bears tiny purple bells on wiry stems. Give it sun and even moisture for a fine-textured mat of bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow near the last frost; the pointed, fringed petals give a starry look on compact plants.

directions: Press the small seed shallowly into the soil surface and cover just enough to shade it, keeping temperatures on the cool side. Space the dwarf plants close for a solid carpet of star-shaped blooms with contrasting eyes. Shear lightly after the first flush to prompt a fresh round of color.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost for its bicolor lavender-and-white pointed petals.

directions: Scatter seed thinly over prepared soil and barely cover, then keep moist until sprouts appear in a couple of weeks. Thin to give each plant room and grow in full sun for the crispest petal markings. Remove faded heads regularly and the frosted star flowers will keep opening for weeks.

planting.note: Start indoors for this tall cut-flower type prized for creamy caramel and cherry tones.

directions: Start seed indoors in cool conditions, sowing shallowly and keeping the tray dark until germination. Move sturdy seedlings out after frost danger and pinch once for a fuller, better-branched cut-flower plant. Harvest stems when about half the flowers on the head have opened for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Start indoors; a tall floret mix in soft custard, buff, and dusky rose shades.

directions: Sow seed indoors on the surface of moist mix and exclude light until the seedlings break through. Grow on cool and transplant after the last frost, spacing plants for good airflow. Cut long stems in the early morning as the head begins to open, and expect a fresh flush if you keep cutting.

planting.note: Start indoors for a pastel blend of buff, apricot, cream, and soft pink.

directions: Sow shallowly in trays and keep the surface dark and cool while seed sprouts over two to three weeks. Set plants out after frost and pinch the tips once to build a bushier stand. Deadhead often, or cut for the vase, and the muted pastel heads will bloom through the cooler months.

planting.note: Start indoors for this fragrant tall mix in smoky, antique color blends.

directions: Sow seed just below the surface and keep it shaded and evenly damp until germination. Harden off and plant out once frosts have passed, giving each plant room for air to move. Enjoy the sweet evening scent and cut freely, since regular harvest keeps the muted heads coming.

planting.note: An heirloom selection with pink buds opening to white-edged bicolor blooms.

directions: Direct sow around the last frost, barely covering the seed and keeping the bed cool and moist. Thin the seedlings a hand's width apart once they have a few true leaves. Pinch and deadhead through the season to keep the charming pink-and-white heads opening.

planting.note: Start indoors for shifting blue, lavender, and violet shades on one plant.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface of a cool, moist mix and keep it dark until sprouts show. Transplant after the last frost and pinch once for extra branching. Cut or deadhead often and the flowers will drift through blue and mauve tones as they age.

planting.note: A pastel mix of coral, salmon, cream, and soft pink shades.

directions: Sow seed shallowly at last frost and cover only lightly, since it germinates best in the dark. Thin to give plants elbow room and grow in full sun for the truest pastel colors. Remove spent heads to prolong the display of gentle coral and cream tones.

planting.note: A compact bedding series in bright, clear single colors.

directions: Start seed indoors in cool, dark conditions, barely covering it in the tray. Move the tidy dwarf plants out after frost and space them close for a dense bedding sheet. Trim after the first flush and the low mounds will rebloom for weeks.

planting.note: A uniform, compact bedding and pack series in a wide color range.

directions: Sow indoors into a cool, moist medium and cover lightly to keep light out during germination. Grow on and transplant after frost for an even, well-branched stand. Deadhead lightly and the plants will hold their bright color through the cooler part of the season.

planting.note: A very compact, early series bred for packs and small pots.

directions: Start seed indoors, pressing it onto moist mix and keeping the surface dark and cool. Transplant into beds or containers after the last frost, spacing plants closely. Pinch spent heads to keep the neat, early-flowering mounds tidy and blooming.

planting.note: An heirloom form in an unusual soft primrose-yellow.

directions: Direct sow near the last frost and cover the seed only thinly, keeping the bed cool. Thin seedlings to a hand's width and grow in full sun with steady moisture. Deadhead to extend the run of pale, buttery blooms that fade toward cream.

planting.note: An old carmine-rose selection with a pale contrasting eye.

directions: Sow shallowly around the last frost and cover just enough to darken the seed. Thin the young plants and grow them in full sun for the strongest color. Snip away spent clusters and the vivid rose flowers will keep coming into summer.

planting.note: A classic deep rose-pink form with a bright white eye.

directions: Direct sow at the last frost, barely covering the seed to allow the darkness it prefers. Thin to give each plant space and keep the soil evenly moist while young. Deadhead regularly and the eye-catching rose-and-white blooms will last for weeks.

planting.note: A double-flowered pink form whose blooms resemble tiny roses.

directions: Sow indoors on cool, moist mix and keep the surface dark until seedlings appear. Transplant after frost and grow in full sun for the fullest double flowers. Deadhead the little rosette blooms often to keep the plant producing.

planting.note: A star-shaped mix with deeply cut, pointed petals in many colors.

directions: Sow shallowly at the last frost and barely cover the seed to keep it dark. Thin the compact plants close together for a mass of spiky star flowers. Shear back after the first flush to trigger fresh bloom.

planting.note: A pastel series in gentle, muted color blends.

directions: Start seed indoors on a cool, moist surface kept dark until germination. Transplant after frost and pinch once for a bushier plant. Deadhead through the season to hold the soft pastel display.

planting.note: A large-flowered heirloom strain in a broad mix of colors.

directions: Direct sow around the last frost, covering the seed only lightly for the darkness it needs. Thin to a hand's width and grow in full sun with steady moisture for big, rounded heads. Deadhead often to keep the large blooms coming through summer.

planting.note: The big-bloomed strain with larger individual florets than the type.

directions: Sow shallowly near the last frost and barely cover the seed, keeping the bed cool. Thin the plants for good air movement and grow in full sun. Remove faded heads to encourage a long run of the oversized flowers.

planting.note: The low, dense dwarf strain suited to edging and containers.

directions: Direct sow at the last frost and cover the seed only enough to shade it. Thin the little plants close together for a solid, low band of color. Shear after the first flush and the compact mounds will flower again.

planting.note: The pointed-petal botanical variety that gives the star-flowered forms.

directions: Sow seed shallowly near the last frost and keep it dark and cool while it germinates. Thin the seedlings and grow in full sun for the crispest star-shaped petals. Deadhead through the season to keep the pointed blooms opening.

planting.note: A dwarf mix in bright colors, many with contrasting eyes.

directions: Direct sow at the last frost, barely covering the seed to keep light off it. Thin the compact plants close for a colorful, low bedding effect. Deadhead lightly and the bright, dark-eyed flowers will bloom on for weeks.

planting.note: A rounded, ball-shaped dwarf series for packs and edging.

directions: Start seed indoors in cool, dark conditions and cover it only lightly. Transplant after the last frost, spacing plants close for a neat, domed habit. Trim spent heads to keep the little globes flowering.

planting.note: A compact bedding series, many colors carrying a contrasting eye.

directions: Sow indoors onto moist mix and keep the surface dark until seedlings emerge. Grow on cool and transplant after frost for an even, tidy stand. Deadhead through the season and the eyed flowers will keep their bright display.

planting.note: Start seed indoors under bright light, then move plants outdoors after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed in warm cells and leave it uncovered, misting gently to settle it in. This day-neutral strain forms tidy mounds of fully double blooms and opens earlier in the day than older types. Set hardened plants into lean, fast-draining soil after the last frost and expect steady color all summer.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out when nights stay warm.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface of warm starting mix and press lightly without burying it. The double flowers open a soft peach shade and stay open longer in the day than heirloom moss rose. Give it full sun and dry, sandy ground, then watch for the first buds about eight weeks from sowing.

planting.note: Begin seed under lights and transplant after frost danger ends.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a warm, well-drained mix and keep it out of standing water. The rose-form blooms glow in warm orange-gold tones and shrug off heat and drought once rooted. Plant into full sun after the ground warms, and enjoy nonstop bloom from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors in warmth, then set out once frost is past.

directions: Press the seed lightly onto warm starter mix and leave it exposed to light to sprout. Expect bright magenta double flowers on low, spreading succulent stems that thrive on neglect. Transplant into gritty soil in a hot, sunny bed and water only when the ground is dry.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and move them out after the last frost.

directions: Sprinkle the fine seed over the surface and mist rather than cover it. The clear yellow double blooms cover fleshy, needle-like foliage and open wide in bright sun. Plant into poor, sharply drained soil and let it bake; overwatering is the main way to lose it.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant into warm ground after frost.

directions: Set the seed on the surface of a warm, gritty mix and keep it uncovered under bright light. The crisp white rosette flowers brighten hot borders and edging where little else thrives. Give it full sun and dry roots, and pinch off nothing, as it reblooms freely on its own.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out when the soil is warm.

directions: Press the seed lightly into warm starting mix and do not bury it. Soft pink double flowers open each sunny morning above low, trailing succulent growth. Move plants to a hot, well-drained spot after frost and water only during long dry spells.

planting.note: Start seed indoors under lights and set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on warm mix and leave it uncovered to catch the light. This vigorous, well-branched strain carries large double blooms in a broad range of hot colors. Give it full sun and dry, gritty soil, and expect masses of flowers within about eight weeks of sowing.

planting.note: Grow indoors early, then transplant once nights are warm.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface of a warm tray and press gently without covering. Deep rose-pink double flowers cover the spreading, fleshy stems all season. Plant into lean, well-drained soil in full sun and hold back on water once established.

planting.note: Start under lights and plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Press the seed onto warm starting mix and leave it exposed to light to sprout. Pure white double blooms sit above low mounds of succulent, cylindrical leaves. Give it a hot, sunny bed with sharp drainage and let the soil dry between waterings.

planting.note: Raise indoors early and transplant into warm soil after frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and mist to settle the seed without burying it. Bright yellow double flowers open wide on sunny days over spreading succulent growth. Set plants in full sun and dry, gritty ground, and avoid rich soil that leads to floppy stems.

common_name: Happy Hour Deep Red Moss Rose

planting.note: Start seedlings indoors and move them out after frost.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto warm mix and leave it uncovered for light. Rich, saturated red double blooms make a bold ribbon along hot edges and containers. Plant into full sun with fast drainage and water only when the soil has fully dried.

planting.note: Begin indoors under lights, then transplant after frost.

directions: Sow onto the surface of a warm tray and keep the seed exposed to bright light. Vivid orange double flowers cover the low mounds and open reliably in sun. Give it a baking, well-drained site and go light on the watering can once it roots.

planting.note: Start indoors and set plants out once the ground warms.

directions: Press the seed lightly onto warm starting mix and leave it uncovered. Electric fuchsia-pink double blooms sit above fleshy foliage and love reflected heat. Transplant into gritty, sun-baked soil after frost and keep water to a minimum.

planting.note: Direct-sow once the soil has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Broadcast the seed on warm, well-drained soil and press it in without covering. This semi-trailing mix carries large, semi-double flowers in a wide blend of bright shades. Thin seedlings to a hand's width apart and expect the first blooms about two months from sowing.

planting.note: Sow directly into warm ground after frost danger passes.

directions: Scatter the fine seed over warm soil and leave it on the surface to catch light. This double-flowered blend mixes rose, gold, orange, and white on low, spreading plants. Keep the bed barely moist until germination, then let it dry out and bloom through summer heat.

planting.note: Direct-sow after the last frost when soil is reliably warm.

directions: Press the seed lightly onto warm soil and do not bury it, since it needs light to sprout. This older strain was selected to hold its double flowers open even on dull, cloudy days. Give it full sun and lean, sandy ground, and thin the seedlings so plants can spread freely.

planting.note: Start indoors under lights and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and leave the seed uncovered under bright light. This compact, well-branched series flowers early with large double blooms in mixed colors. Move hardened plants to a hot, sunny bed in gritty soil and water only when it dries out.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out once nights are warm and frost is past.

directions: This vegetative selection is grown from rooted cuttings rather than seed, so start with young plants. Its bright fuchsia flowers stay open longer through the day than seed-grown moss rose. Plant into gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun, let it trail, and water only when the mix dries out.

planting.note: Transplant rooted starts into warm ground after frost.

directions: Grown from cuttings, this trailing selection is sold as young plants rather than seed packets. Clear yellow single flowers cover the low, spreading stems all summer in the heat. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and hold back water to keep the succulent growth firm.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out once the soil is warm and frost-free.

directions: This vegetative variety comes as rooted starts, since cuttings root faster than seed germinates. Deep red single blooms trail over the edges of pots, baskets, and hot borders. Set it in full sun with gritty soil, and treat it like a succulent by watering sparingly.

planting.note: Move rooted plants outdoors after the danger of frost ends.

directions: Sold as young rooted plants, this cutting-grown selection skips the seed stage entirely. Warm tangerine-orange flowers glow above trailing, fleshy stems in full sun. Plant into lean, well-drained soil, let it cascade, and water only when the surface is dry.

planting.note: Set rooted starts out into warm soil after frost.

directions: This trailing type is propagated from cuttings and offered as young plants in spring. Soft pink single flowers open across the low, spreading mat during hot weather. Grow it in full sun with fast-draining soil, and keep watering light to avoid rot.

planting.note: Plant rooted starts outdoors once nights stay warm.

directions: This cutting-grown series is sold as vigorous young plants rather than seed. Large coral-toned flowers hold up well in heat and humidity on mounding, trailing stems. Give it full sun and gritty soil, then water sparingly to keep the plant compact and blooming.

planting.note: Transplant rooted plants after frost when soil is warm.

directions: Offered as rooted young plants, this vegetative variety is not grown from seed at home. Bright yellow flowers cover the heat-loving, semi-trailing mounds all summer long. Set it in full sun with sharp drainage and let the soil dry between light waterings.

planting.note: Sow into warm soil after frost, or set out rooted cuttings.

directions: Press the small seed onto warm, well-drained soil and leave it on the surface to sprout. This low, trailing succulent bears fleshy paddle-shaped leaves and cheerful cup-shaped flowers. Grow it in hot, dry, sunny spots where it spreads into a drought-proof groundcover and blooms into autumn.

planting.note: Sow indoors eight to four weeks before the last frost for the earliest large blooms.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm moist mix and leave the seed uncovered under bright light. Transplant hardened seedlings after frost, spacing them well to support the huge six-to-nine-inch golden daisies. Cut for the vase or deadhead often, and the branching plants keep flowering until frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors early because double forms take a little longer to reach bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly onto warm mix and keep it in bright light until it germinates. Set plants out after frost in full sun with room to branch. Expect a mix of double and semi-double blooms in bronze, rust, gold, and mahogany from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of the last frost for tall midsummer stems.

directions: Leave the seed uncovered on moist mix and give it bright, warm conditions to sprout. Transplant after danger of frost into full sun. The golden petals surround a soft green central cone that makes an excellent cut flower; snip stems freely to encourage more.

planting.note: Start indoors for early color; the compact habit suits pots and edges.

directions: Sow on the surface of warm mix and keep it lit and lightly moist. Move the tidy seedlings to containers or the front of a border after frost. This dwarf pours out golden-yellow daisies on short sturdy stems; pinch off old heads to keep it neat and flowering.

planting.note: Best started indoors; ideal for containers and small-space gardens.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix without covering and keep warm under bright light. Transplant compact plants after frost into pots or border fronts. The blooms blend gold with warm mahogany bands around a dark cone; regular deadheading keeps the dwarf plant flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors for the earliest soft-yellow blooms in pots or edging.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed on warm mix and leave it exposed to light. Set the short plants out after frost in full sun. Its pale lemon-yellow petals ring a chocolate cone on a very compact frame; remove spent heads to prolong the display.

planting.note: Start indoors for large single blooms by early summer.

directions: Scatter seed on moist starting mix and press gently without burying. After frost move plants to full sun and average, well-drained soil. Each large single flower shifts through gold, bronze, and rust tones around a dark eye; deadhead to keep blooms coming all season.

planting.note: Start indoors or sow directly after the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Sow on the surface and firm the seed into contact with damp soil or mix. Thin or space plants a foot or more apart in full sun. Expect a lively mix of gold, bronze, and mahogany bicolors on shorter, weather-tough plants; snip old heads to keep them blooming.

planting.note: Start indoors for a strong first-year flush of big blooms.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm moist mix under bright light and do not cover. Plant out after frost into full sun and well-drained ground. The large golden petals carry a bold mahogany-red zone around the cone; deadhead regularly for continuous flowering into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors so the quilled blooms have a long season to perform.

directions: Leave the seed uncovered on damp mix and germinate it warm and bright. Transplant after frost into full sun with good airflow. The narrow, rolled quill-like petals span gold, bronze, russet, and mahogany; cut stems for arrangements or deadhead to keep flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors; the compact double is well suited to containers.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of warm mix and keep it moist under bright light. Set plants out after frost into full sun and pots or border fronts. The fully double, pompon-like golden blooms sit on short bushy plants; remove faded flowers to keep the show going.

planting.note: Start indoors for large bicolor blooms on a low plant.

directions: Surface-sow and keep the seed warm, moist, and exposed to light. Plant out after frost in full sun with well-drained soil. Each big golden flower carries a broad mahogany blotch at the petal base around a dark eye; deadhead to keep the compact plant blooming.

planting.note: Start indoors for early, uniform golden-orange bloom.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of warm mix and leave it uncovered in bright light. Transplant after frost into full sun and average soil. This classic bears masses of deep golden-orange single daisies with dark centers; frequent deadheading keeps flowers coming through summer.

planting.note: Start indoors for double and semi-double flowers on a compact plant.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist mix and keep warm under good light. Move plants outdoors after frost into full sun. The gold, double to semi-double blooms are held on tidy, well-branched plants; deadhead to extend flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors; this very dwarf mix is made for pots and edges.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on warm mix and keep it lit and evenly moist. Transplant the compact seedlings after frost into containers or border fronts. Flowers arrive in a blend of gold, orange, lemon, and mahogany bicolors low to the ground; deadhead to keep them tidy and blooming.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct-sow after frost for large mixed single blooms.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface and keep it damp until sprouting, leaving it uncovered. Thin or space plants well in full sun to support the tall stems. The oversized single daisies range across gold, bronze, and mahogany zones; deadhead or cut for the vase to keep flowers forming.

planting.note: Start indoors for the muted vintage-toned blooms by midsummer.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm moist mix and germinate under bright light. Plant out after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. The blooms open in soft, dusky shades of rose, caramel, bronze, and cream; cut freely for arrangements and deadhead to prolong the display.

planting.note: Start indoors for well-branched plants covered in bicolor flowers.

directions: Leave the seed on the surface of moist mix and keep it warm and lit. Transplant after frost into full sun. The large flowers blend gold petals with rich mahogany-bronze centers and rings in an ever-changing mix; deadhead often to keep the plant flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors for uniform, quick-flowering plants ideal for bedding.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm mix and keep it bright and evenly moist. Plant out after frost into full sun and average soil. This uniform selection produces a tidy, dense cover of golden-yellow daisies with dark eyes; deadhead to maintain steady bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors for the deep mahogany-red single blooms.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of damp mix and germinate warm and bright. Transplant after frost into full sun with good drainage. The single flowers open in deep mahogany-red, often edged with gold, around a dark cone; deadhead to keep flowers forming through the season.

planting.note: Start indoors; this is a tall, branching selection for the back of a border.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on warm mix and leave it uncovered under light. Plant out after frost into full sun with room for the tall stems, and stake in windy sites. The flowers glow red-orange with gold-tipped petals around a dark cone; cut for the vase or deadhead to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors for early, uniform golden bloom on a strong plant.

directions: Sow on the surface of warm mix and keep it moist under bright light. Transplant after frost into full sun and well-drained soil. This vigorous, uniform selection carries golden daisies with dark cones over a long season; deadhead regularly to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors so the double blooms have a full season to develop.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm moist mix and germinate under bright light. Plant out after frost into full sun with average soil. The large, fully double golden flowers form a ruffled dome above dark-green foliage; deadhead to keep new blooms coming.

planting.note: Start indoors early; double gloriosa types are slower to reach bloom.

directions: Press seed onto damp starting mix and keep it warm and uncovered in bright light. Transplant after frost into full sun with room to branch. The big, fully double golden-yellow blooms make long-lasting cut flowers; keep cutting or deadheading to prolong the display.

planting.note: Start indoors for bicolor blooms on a well-branched plant.

directions: Surface-sow seed on warm mix and keep it lit and evenly moist. Plant out after frost into full sun and free-draining soil. The flowers combine gold outer petals with warm bronze-mahogany centers around a dark eye; deadhead to keep the plant blooming into fall.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and transplant compact seedlings after frost danger passes.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed, exclude light for germination, and keep the seedlings cool and evenly moist. This dwarf, well-branched strain needs little staking and blends golds, reds, purples, and chocolate tones on one planting. Watch for the marbled, veined blooms to open about three months after sowing.

planting.note: Begin indoors about eight weeks ahead of the last frost and set out hardened seedlings.

directions: Press seed onto the surface, cover to darken, and germinate warm before growing on in bright cool conditions. The compact plants carry deep cocoa-brown trumpets netted with gold veining that suit containers and cut arrangements. Blooms appear roughly three months from sowing and last longer if spent flowers are removed.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors in early spring and plant out when settled warmth arrives.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on firmed moist mix and keep it dark until sprouts show. This tidy selection produces golden-yellow trumpets traced with fine bronze veins on self-supporting stems. First flowers open about three months after sowing and repeat through the warm season.

planting.note: Start under cover late winter and transplant once frosts have finished.

directions: Surface-sow and cover the container to block light, which this species needs to sprout. The dwarf, freely branching plants bear rich purple trumpets flushed and veined with contrasting gold. Look for bloom about three months from sowing and deadhead to keep new buds coming.

planting.note: Sow indoors two months before last frost and harden off before setting out.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix, darken the tray, and germinate at warm room temperature. This selection is prized for its deep indigo to violet-blue trumpets laced with golden veining, striking against fine mid-green foliage. Provide light support in exposed spots and expect flowers about three to four months from sowing.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors in early spring and move outdoors after the last frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of moist compost and exclude light until germination. This early, weather-tolerant mix stays fairly compact and offers a full spectrum of veined jewel tones with less need for staking. Blooms begin roughly three months after sowing and carry on into early autumn with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Start indoors about eight weeks before last frost and plant out into a sheltered sunny bed.

directions: Firm the mix, press the seed onto the surface, and cover to darken for germination. This taller mixture yields large trumpet flowers in a wide range of veined colors and makes an excellent cut flower. Stake in windy gardens and expect the first blooms about three to four months from sowing.

planting.note: Sow the F1 seed indoors in late winter and transplant after frosts end.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and keep dark and warm until the fine seedlings emerge. This uniform F1 hybrid flowers freely and early with heavily veined blooms across the full color range. Grow cool and bright before planting out, and look for the display to begin about three months from sowing.

planting.note: Begin indoors about two months before last frost and set out well-hardened plants.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto firmed damp compost and exclude light to prompt germination. This classic tall strain carries large ruffled trumpets with bold contrasting veins on upright stems that benefit from support. It excels as a cut flower, with the first blooms opening about three to four months after sowing.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it lit and warm until sprouts appear in a couple of weeks. This compact, early strain holds a tidy mound perfect for the front of a bed or a container. Bright red spikes cover the plant from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Start indoors and move outdoors once nights stay warm.

directions: Sprinkle seed on the surface of a warm, moist mix and give it good light to germinate. This dwarf series blooms in a blend of red, salmon, purple, white, and bicolor shades on uniform plants. Expect steady spikes from midsummer until frost with occasional deadheading.

planting.note: Sow indoors early; often grown as an annual outside its hardy range.

directions: Start seed indoors under bright light and transplant after the ground warms. This tough Texas native shrugs off heat and drought once established and rarely needs fussing. Slender violet-blue spikes with a mealy silver cast bloom from early summer to frost and draw bees.

planting.note: Begin indoors and set out after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep warm and bright until seedlings emerge. This popular strain forms upright deep-blue spikes prized for cutting and drying. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the flowers coming from summer into fall.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out after frost.

directions: Start seed on the surface of a warm mix with good light. This white-flowered form pairs beautifully with blue salvias and other summer annuals. Trim faded spikes to encourage a long bloom run from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors for early bloom or direct-sow after frost.

directions: Press seed onto warm, moist soil and keep it lit until sprouts appear. This compact All-America Selections winner blooms remarkably early and stays neat. Small red spikes bring in hummingbirds and pollinators from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors or sow directly once warm.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on warm soil and keep evenly moist for germination. This dwarf, early-blooming winner carries soft pink spikes on compact plants. Deadheading is optional since it blooms freely from summer into fall.

planting.note: Raise indoors or direct-sow after frost.

directions: Barely press seed into a warm, moist mix and provide light to sprout. This compact white selection lights up beds and mixes well with the red and pink forms. Clean white spikes bloom early and hold until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct-sow when warm.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on warm soil and keep it lightly damp until germination. This selection contrasts dark stems and calyces against fiery red blooms. It flowers from summer to frost and self-sows in mild gardens.

planting.note: Direct-sow around the last frost; tolerates cool starts.

directions: Sow seed shallowly where plants are to grow and thin once seedlings are up. The showy color comes from papery bracts, not the tiny true flowers, and holds for weeks. Cut stems for fresh or dried bouquets when the bracts are fully colored in summer.

planting.note: Direct-sow near the last frost date.

directions: Scatter seed thinly over the bed and cover lightly, then thin as seedlings grow. This strain shows off deep violet-blue bracts that keep their color when dried. Harvest stems for arrangements once the bracts have colored fully.

planting.note: Direct-sow around the last frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and keep the bed moist until seedlings appear. This form carries creamy white bracts veined in green for a soft cottage look. Cut stems for fresh or dried use when the bracts are well developed.

planting.note: Raise indoors and set out after frost; overwinter tubers frost-free.

directions: Sow indoors in warmth and grow on before planting out after frost. This selection softens the species with pale sky-blue flowers on upright stems. Blooms continue from summer into autumn, and the tubers can be stored dry for winter.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep warm and bright to germinate. This early dwarf series comes in a mix of red, salmon, lavender, burgundy, and white. Compact plants bloom fast and stay tidy in beds and containers until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep warm and lit until germination. This white counterpart to Victoria Blue makes airy spikes good for cutting. Deadhead to keep blooms coming through summer and fall.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out once soil is warm.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and provide warmth and light until sprouts show. This taller heirloom-type strain carries bold scarlet spikes above deep green leaves. It blooms from early summer to frost and shines massed in beds.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct-sow after frost.

directions: Barely cover seed on warm soil and keep lightly moist to germinate. This pure white form of tropical sage lights up beds and self-sows gently. Airy white spikes bloom from summer to frost and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Direct-sow near the last frost date.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin seedlings as they establish. This selection carries rosy-pink bracts that hold color for fresh and dried bouquets. Cut stems once the bracts have colored well in summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Barely cover the seed since light aids germination, and keep the surface evenly moist until sprouts appear. Grow in lean, well-drained soil in full sun and avoid overwatering, as this heat-lover resents soggy roots. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching; small golden daisies with dark button centers begin in early summer and carry on until frost.
